topics: 🏺 The potter who left pots in someone else's courtyard without permission
🌾 Pots, produce, and bulls — the rule of the joint courtyard
🐂 With permission vs without permission — who absorbs the loss
🤝 Rebbi: only liable if he explicitly accepted to watch
🤰 Bull intending to gore — but a pregnant woman miscarries

overview.title: 📖 Overview — The Courtyard, the Pots, and the Pregnant Woman
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: Daf 24 walks through three Mishnayot of Chapter 5 that share a single structural test: was it brought in with permission? The answer flips liability between the courtyard-owner and the item-owner. The daf closes by pivoting to the unintentional miscarriage — and to the question of whether a fetus can be freed by 'tooth and eye'.
overview.hebrewSource: הִכְנִיס פֵּירוֹתָיו לַחֲצַר בַּעַל הַבַּיִת שֶׁלֹּא בִּרְשׁוּת... וְאִם הוּזְּקָה בָהֶן בַּעַל הַפֵּירוֹת חַיָיב.

slides.title: 📊 The Three Items in the Courtyard
slides.subtitle: Same logic, three different items.
headers: Without permission — animal hurt by it
rows: Owner of bayit — פטור (gored), חייב (his bull gored)
Owner of bull — חייב for damages caused

callout.label: Yerushalmi conclusion:
callout.text: When neither party owns the courtyard, even קרן and שן damages don't apply: הקרן פטורה בחצר שאינה לשניהם.

slides.title: 🤝 Rebbi vs the Sages — What 'With Permission' Means
slides.subtitle: R' Yirmiyah maps where everyone agrees and where they fight.

boxes.title: 🏡 Courtyard
items: Rebbi: courtyard is like a field
Sages: courtyard is like a house

boxes.title: 🔒 Special case
items: R' Yose b' R' Bun: courtyard with a lock
Lock implies guarding is on the owner

callout.label: Halakhah:
callout.text: Rav Yehuda in Shmuel's name and Rav Ze'ira: הלכה כרבי. But Rebbi himself adds: 'in all of these — only if he explicitly accepted to watch.'

slides.title: 🐂 The Bull Gored a Bull or Bit a Person
slides.subtitle: Five scenarios when his bull is in the host's courtyard without permission.

nodes.content: הכניס שורו לחצר בעל הבית שלא ברשות
Father or son was inside? — משלם את הכופר
With permission: bayit owner liable. Rebbi: only if he explicitly accepted to watch.

callout.label: Sages dispute:
callout.text: R' Meir: even from a third bull, full damages. Sages: only שן and רגל are full; קרן/נגיפה/etc. are tam-half or muad-full from עלייה.

slides.title: 💬 The Pregnant Woman Caught in the Crossfire
slides.subtitle: A bull or a man intends to hit someone — and a pregnant woman miscarries.

lines.speaker: M
lines.text: Mishnah: a bull aiming at another bull, hit a woman, fetus lost — פטור מדמי וולדות.

lines.speaker: M2
lines.text: But: a man aiming at a friend, hit a pregnant woman — משלם דמי וולדות to the husband.

lines.speaker: AB
lines.text: Abba bar Rav Hana: the bull case is taught only as setup for the human case — bulls never pay fetus damages either way.

lines.speaker: ABY
lines.text: Abba Yose ben Hanan: 'הרה' teaches he must hit the place of pregnancy. If he hit hand or foot — פטור.

lines.speaker: RU
lines.text: R' Uqba: what if it's incest — is the perpetrator a 'בעל'? The verse says 'בעל' — only one who could be called husband.

callout.label: Drasha:
callout.text: From כי ינצו אנשים — humans not bulls. The Yerushalmi reads each word of Exodus 21:22 to filter who pays whom.

slides.title: 👁️ Fetuses and 'Tooth and Eye'
slides.subtitle: R' Yasa's rule about freeing a Canaanite slave's fetus.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Canaanite slave goes free if owner knocks out his tooth or eye.
2️⃣ R' Yasa in R' Yochanan's name: עוברין אין יוצאין בשן ועין.
3️⃣ Question: ניחא בשן — but how does an unborn fetus have an 'eye' to lose?
4️⃣ R' Yose b' R' Bun: the baraita means — fetuses don't go free by their mother's tooth and eye.
5️⃣ Therefore: damage to the mother does not propagate freedom to the fetus.

callout.label: Bottom line:
callout.text: Daf 24 closes one principle and opens another: ownership and intention determine liability — but separate persons (mother / fetus) follow separate emancipation rules.

summary.principle: Permission to enter a courtyard transfers the duty of watching from one party to the other; without permission, the intruder bears the loss and any damages he causes.

columns.content:
🐂 His own animal
still pays for damage caused

flowSteps.content: The Yerushalmi tracks the inversion: same scenario, opposite liability based on permission.

🎓 Key Principle:
The verbal grant 'come in' is a transfer of guarding duty — and intent determines who pays for fetuses.

quiz.q: If pots are placed without permission and an animal of the bayit owner breaks them, who is liable?
o: Nobody — the bayit owner is פטור
The pot owner pays the bayit owner
quiz.e: Without permission, the bayit owner is exempt; if the animal was hurt by the pots, the pot owner is liable.

quiz.q: Where does R' Yirmiyah place the dispute between Rebbi and the Sages?
quiz.e: Everyone agrees about house and field; the dispute is about a courtyard — Rebbi: like a field; Sages: like a house.

quiz.q: Why does the Mishnah teach the bull-aiming-at-bull case?
o: To make the bull liable for fetuses
Only as a setup to teach the contrasting human case (Abba bar Rav Hana)
quiz.e: Abba bar Rav Hana: bulls never pay fetus damages; the case is taught to set up the human case where intent transfers.

quiz.q: What does Abba Yose ben Hanan derive from the word 'הרה'?
o: He must hit the place of pregnancy or he is פטור
quiz.e: If he struck her hand or foot rather than the area of pregnancy, he is exempt from fetus payment.

quiz.q: What does R' Yasa say about freeing fetuses by tooth and eye?
o: A fetus is freed when its mother is freed
Fetuses do not go free by their mother's tooth and eye
quiz.e: עוברין אין יוצאין בשן ועין של אמן — damage to the mother doesn't propagate freedom to her fetus.
